Row1.Pathway ID/Name: ALK oncogenic signaling (EML4–ALK → RAS–MAPK / PI3K–AKT / JAK–STAT)
Row1.Rationale: Lorlatinib is a brain‑penetrant third‑generation ALK/ROS1 TKI with potent activity against wild‑type ALK and most single resistance mutations, including G1202R; inhibition of ALK signaling underlies the high systemic and intracranial response rates and prolonged PFS seen in ALK‑positive NSCLC, particularly when used first line in CROWN.

Row2.Pathway ID/Name: ALK kinase domain single mutations (e.g., L1196M, G1269A, F1174X, D1203N)
Row2.Rationale: Many secondary ALK point mutations that confer resistance to first‑/second‑generation TKIs remain sensitive to lorlatinib; phase II data show substantial response rates in post‑crizotinib and post‑2G ALK‑TKI cohorts, and CROWN ctDNA analyses indicate broad coverage of single ALK kinase‑domain mutants.

Row3.Pathway ID/Name: ALK compound mutation network (e.g., G1202R/L1196M, G1202R/F1174C, L1196M/D1203N, C1156Y/G1269A)
Row3.Rationale: Sequential exposure to 1G/2G TKIs then lorlatinib selects for double/triple ALK kinase mutations; in vitro and clinical series show G1202R‑based and L1196M/D1203N compound mutants are highly refractory to all approved ALK TKIs including lorlatinib, whereas a subset (e.g., C1156Y/L1198F, I1171N/L1198F) paradoxically re‑sensitize tumors to earlier‑generation inhibitors.

Row4.Rationale: Off‑target resistance frequently converges on PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling: NF2 loss and other alterations activate this pathway in ALK‑rearranged NSCLC, and in first‑line lorlatinib‑treated patients from CROWN, ctDNA at progression showed enrichment of PI3K/MTOR/PTEN pathway alterations without new ALK mutations, indicating ALK‑independent survival signaling.

Row5.Rationale: ALK fusions drive MAPK signaling, and multiple reviews and clinicogenomic studies highlight MAPK‑pathway alterations (e.g., KRAS, MAPK components) as common in advanced and brain‑metastatic NSCLC; under lorlatinib pressure, bypass RTKs and downstream MAPK reactivation provide an ALK‑independent proliferation route.

Row6.Pathway ID/Name: RTK bypass signaling (EGFR / MET and other RTKs)
Row6.Rationale: Activation or amplification of alternative RTKs such as EGFR and MET has been repeatedly described as ALK‑independent resistance to ALK TKIs; lorlatinib‑resistant models and clinical series show EGFR‑pathway activation and other RTK alterations that maintain MAPK and PI3K signaling despite ALK blockade.

Row7.Rationale: Activation of YAP, the key Hippo effector encoded by YAP1, has been implicated as an ALK‑independent resistance mechanism; overexpression of YAP reduces sensitivity to ALK TKIs in preclinical ALK‑rearranged models, and gene‑level data support YAP1 as a transcriptional co‑activator capable of driving survival programs when ALK is inhibited.

Row8.Rationale: Reviews and case reports of lorlatinib resistance note roles for epithelial‑to‑mesenchymal transition (EMT) and histologic transformation (e.g., to small‑cell phenotype) as ALK‑independent escape routes; these programs decouple tumor survival from ALK signaling and are associated with poor chemotherapy responsiveness after lorlatinib failure.

Row9.Pathway ID/Name: Cell‑cycle checkpoint pathways (TP53/RB1/STK11 and CDKN2A axis)
Row9.Rationale: Co‑alterations in TP53, RB1, STK11 and CDKN2A are frequent in NSCLC and brain metastases and correlate with worse outcomes and TKI resistance; loss of these tumor suppressors promotes proliferation, genomic instability, and lineage plasticity, reducing dependence on single‑driver ALK signaling under lorlatinib.

Row10.Rationale: Multi‑omics studies show recurrent alterations in genes such as ATM, KEAP1, SETD2, ARID1A and SMARCA4 in advanced and brain‑metastatic NSCLC; these changes enhance adaptability and may facilitate acquisition of complex ALK mutations and off‑target resistance pathways during prolonged lorlatinib exposure.

Row11.Pathway ID/Name: CNS metastasis–associated pathways (TP53/MAPK/PI3K and YAP1 networks)
Row11.Rationale: Lorlatinib provides deep intracranial control, yet brain‑metastasis specimens from NSCLC are enriched for alterations in TP53, MAPK, PI3K/mTOR, cell‑cycle genes and YAP1; these networks may underlie CNS tropism and influence the biology of residual or emergent brain disease despite effective ALK inhibition.

Row12.Rationale: Liquid‑biopsy and multi‑omics reviews emphasize that drug‑tolerant persister cells, immune‑evasive microenvironments, and macrophage‑driven immunosuppression enable survival under targeted therapies; such non‑genomic and immunologic adaptations likely contribute to lorlatinib resistance when no ALK or clear bypass alterations are detected.
